title: Adversarial Repackaging Is Enough
subtitle: Gaming AI Peer Review Without Changing the Science
thesis: Editing only how a paper is presented (its abstract, contribution statements, and narrative) while every dataset, method, and number stays fixed, can systematically overturn an AI reviewer's verdict.
abstract: As paper submissions grow against a shortage of qualified reviewers, LLM-generated reviews are rapidly entering peer review for their efficiency, yet robustness discussions focus primarily on explicit attacks such as prompt injection. We identify a more subtle and policy-relevant risk: modifying only presentation-level content (abstracts, contribution statements, and narrative structure, with scientific content held fixed) can systematically overturn AI review outcomes. We propose resistance to presentation-only review gaming as a necessary condition for AI review automation, and test it via adversarial repackaging: iteratively searching presentation-level edits using the AI reviewer's own feedback. Across three mainstream models, the attack achieves a 75.1% success rate with a mean score gain of +1.21/10. Further analysis reveals structural deficiencies: a strength–weakness asymmetry, where highlighting strengths is easier and more reliable while attempts to dissolve criticisms frequently backfire; and a strategy effectiveness gradient, where edits changing how the reviewer understands the paper far outperform those improving surface appearance. This vulnerability is consistent across models and review templates: not a fixable single-model issue but a structural deficiency that could distort the incentive structure of peer review. We release a contamination-free dataset and attack framework as a reusable benchmark.
